title: Running Low-Latency Analytical Workloads with GPU-Accelerated Presto on NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 | NVIDIA Technical Blog
gist: NVIDIA argues GPU-accelerated Presto (via cuDF/Velox) on DGX B200 and GB200 NVL72 delivers 2.5x-8.2x lower latency on TPC-H-derived analytics than 8-10 node Intel Xeon CPU clusters, with GPUDirect Storage paired to IBM Storage Scale as the key I/O enabler at scale.

insights.claim: GPU-accelerated Presto on a single eight-GPU DGX B200 node delivered 8.2x faster TPC-H-derived query runtimes at ~1 TB (scale factor 1K) than an eight-node cluster of two-socket Intel Xeon 6642Y servers.
insights.speaker: Gregory Kimball
insights.so_what: Signals that a single GPU node can displace an 8-node CPU rack for analytics, compressing datacenter footprint and CPU/server unit demand for these workloads.
insights.quote: Presto GPU running with eight B200 GPUs showed 8.2x faster runtimes compared to an eight-node Presto CPU cluster.
insights.locator: At scale factor 1K (~1 TB data set)

insights.claim: Even a single B200 GPU ran TPC-H-derived scale-factor-1K queries 2.5x faster than an eight-node Intel Xeon CPU Presto cluster.
insights.speaker: Gregory Kimball
insights.so_what: Per-GPU throughput undercuts multi-node CPU economics even at minimum GPU count, strengthening the substitution thesis for GPU over CPU in data analytics.
insights.locator: At scale factor 1K (~1 TB data set)

insights.claim: At ~3 TB (scale factor 3K), eight B200 GPUs beat a 10-node Xeon CPU cluster by 7.8x, and three GPUs beat it by 3.6x, indicating the GPU advantage holds as dataset size scales.
insights.speaker: Gregory Kimball
insights.so_what: GPU lead persists at larger data volumes, supporting a durable rather than small-data-only advantage for analytical query engines.
insights.locator: At scale factor 3K (~3 TB data set)

insights.claim: These are vendor-run benchmarks using source builds of Presto, Velox and cuDF, with runtimes averaged over the last four of five runs and the CPU baseline run hot with Velox async data cache.
insights.speaker: Gregory Kimball
insights.so_what: Benchmarks are NVIDIA-sourced and not independently verified; treat the 8x headline as a vendor best-case, not neutral third-party validation.
insights.locator: This post focuses on benchmarks derived from TPC-H

themes.theme: NVLink and interconnect as the moat

insights.claim: A single DGX B200 node runs eight Presto GPU workers connected over NVLink 5.0 with 1,800 GB/s bidirectional bandwidth, positioning fast GPU-to-GPU interconnect as the enabler of GPU-native analytics.
insights.speaker: Gregory Kimball
insights.so_what: NVLink bandwidth is the differentiator versus commodity networking, reinforcing NVIDIA's system-level lock-in beyond the GPU die itself.
insights.quote: all connected over NVLink 5.0 with 1,800 GB/s bidirectional bandwidth
insights.locator: How does GPU-accelerated Presto deliver peak performance?

insights.claim: The GB200 NVL72 test system comprises 18 nodes, each with two Grace CPUs, four B200 GPUs, and four ConnectX-7 400 Gbps NICs, with all GPUs NVLink-connected across the cluster.
insights.speaker: Gregory Kimball
insights.so_what: Full NVL72 config bundles NVIDIA GPUs, Grace CPUs and ConnectX networking together, so analytics wins pull through NVIDIA's whole hardware stack, not just GPUs.
insights.locator: How does GPU-accelerated Presto scale out on GB200 NVL72?

themes.theme: GPUDirect Storage and I/O bottleneck economics

insights.claim: GPUDirect Storage cold reads ran ~2x faster than POSIX cold reads at two nodes/eight GPUs on NVL72 with IBM Storage Scale, because POSIX incurs bounce-buffer copying and NUMA boundary crossing that GDS bypasses.
insights.speaker: Gregory Kimball
insights.so_what: Storage I/O path, not just compute, gates GPU analytics performance; favors RDMA-capable parallel file systems and topology-aware storage vendors.
insights.quote: GDS cold reads showed ~2x faster runtimes than POSIX cold reads due to POSIX slowdowns from bounce buffer copying and NUMA boundary crossing.
insights.locator: How to achieve faster, lightweight I/O with GDS

insights.claim: Cumulative I/O and communication optimizations (GDS, 16 MiB I/O tasks, 16 I/O threads, rebatching, Q11 rewrite) yielded 64% faster query runtimes during NVL72 cluster bring-up.
insights.speaker: Gregory Kimball
insights.so_what: Headline GPU speedups depend heavily on tuning; out-of-box GPU deployments may materially underperform the marketed numbers until optimized.
insights.quote: Overall the I/O and communication optimizations yielded 64% faster query runtimes.
insights.locator: Figure 2 compares the total query runtime

insights.claim: Rewriting TPC-H Q11 from a SELECT to an INSERT INTO cut its runtime from 50 seconds to 2 seconds by routing results through the GPU parquet writer instead of the coordinator's default HttpExchange, which had held Q11 GPU utilization below 5%.
insights.speaker: Gregory Kimball
insights.so_what: Worker-to-coordinator result transfer is a real bottleneck in GPU Presto today, an unsolved gap that could temper real-world gains until NVIDIA improves it.
insights.quote: reduced runtime on Q11 from 50 seconds to 2 seconds
insights.locator: Q11 rewrite modified the SELECT statement

insights.claim: IBM Storage Scale in the study was not topology-aware for POSIX reads, so those reads paid both a staging-buffer copy and NUMA-crossing penalties that GDS avoids by keeping the CX7-NIC-to-GPU path within one NUMA node.
insights.speaker: Gregory Kimball
insights.so_what: Topology-awareness in storage software is a differentiator; storage vendors lacking RDMA/NUMA-aware paths will bottleneck GPU analytics adoption.
insights.locator: How to achieve faster, lightweight I/O with GDS

insights.claim: GPU-accelerated Presto is integrated into IBM's watsonx.data platform and offered as a technical preview, signaling a productization path via an enterprise data-platform partner rather than NVIDIA-direct only.
insights.speaker: Gregory Kimball
insights.so_what: IBM distribution channel could accelerate enterprise adoption of GPU analytics and expand NVIDIA's datacenter TAM beyond AI training/inference into BI/analytics.

insights.claim: The benchmarked storage backend, IBM Storage Scale, offered 20 storage nodes, 10 PB capacity and ~4.5 TiB/s peak bandwidth, indicating GPU analytics at scale demands high-end parallel storage infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Gregory Kimball
insights.so_what: Adjacent spend on high-bandwidth storage scales with GPU analytics deployments, a tailwind for parallel-filesystem and RDMA-fabric suppliers.
